message.content:
Research how WebGL games and complex canvas applications manage state OUTSIDE of React. Focus on:

1. **Game Engine Patterns:**
   - Entity Component System (ECS)
   - Game loops and frame-based state updates
   - How Three.js/Babylon.js apps manage state
   - Signal-based reactivity (like Preact signals, SolidJS)

2. **WebGL-specific state management:**
   - How do production WebGL apps avoid React re-renders?
   - Direct DOM manipulation vs virtual DOM for canvas
   - requestAnimationFrame integration patterns

3. **External Store Libraries:**
   - Zustand (minimal, works outside React)
   - Jotai (atomic state)
   - Valtio (proxy-based)
   - Which is best for real-time canvas applications?

4. **React Integration:**
   - useSyncExternalStore (React 18 native)
   - How to connect external stores to React without useEffect

Search the web for: "WebGL game state management 2024 2025", "Three.js state management Zustand", "React Three Fiber external store", "useSyncExternalStore real-time", "game loop React integration"

content.text: I'll research state management patterns for WebGL applications and how they integrate with React.

Links: [{"title":"useSyncExternalStore – React","url":"https://react.dev/reference/react/useSyncExternalStore"},{"title":"useSyncExternalStore: Demystified for Practical React Development | Epic React by Kent C. Dodds","url":"https://www.epicreact.dev/use-sync-external-store-demystified-for-practical-react-development-w5ac0"},{"title":"You may be looking for a useSyncExternalStore | Swizec Teller","url":"https://swizec.com/blog/you-may-be-looking-for-a-useSyncExternalStore/"},{"title":"How to use useSyncExternalStore Hook in React ? | by Himasha Wijewickrama | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@himashawije/how-to-use-usesyncexternalstore-hook-in-react-ffd0c784718e"},{"title":"Bridging the Gap - My Journey Understanding React's useSyncExternalStore | Sijo Sam","url":"https://www.sijosam.in/blog/use-sync-external-store/"},{"title":"How useSyncExternalStore() works internally in React?","url":"https://jser.dev/2023-08-02-usesyncexternalstore/"},{"title":"Exploring useSyncExternalStore, a lesser-known React Hook - LogRocket Blog","url":"https://blog.logrocket.com/exploring-usesyncexternalstore-react-hook/"},{"title":"React Hooks: Exploring useSyncExternalStore","url":"https://lo-victoria.com/a-look-at-react-hooks-usesyncexternalstore"},{"title":"Building a Real-Time Online Status Checker with React using useSyncExternalStore - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/sharmaprash/building-a-real-time-online-status-checker-with-react-using-usesyncexternalstore-5k0"},{"title":"Bug: Using an hook in getSnapshot of use-sync-external-store causes an infinite loop and no useful error is shown. · Issue #24529 · facebook/react","url":"https://github.com/facebook/react/issues/24529"}]

Based on my search, here's what I found about `useSyncExternalStore` in the context of real-time applications and React:

## Key Findings

### What is `useSyncExternalStore`?

`useSyncExternalStore` solves the problem of subscribing to external stores by providing a React-managed, synchronous way to subscribe to an external store. It guarantees that all components in a render pass see the same, consistent snapshot of the data, even during concurrent updates.(no content)`useSyncExternalStore` is a hook that was added in React 18. It's used for securely connecting React components to external state sources that are not under React's control.

### Why Use It Instead of useEffect?

While `useEffect` and `useState` can subscribe to external stores, this pattern is prone to tearing in concurrent rendering. React might pause rendering a component, an external store might update, and then React might resume rendering with stale data or cause inconsistencies across the UI.(no content)This hook fires immediately, causing no delays, unlike the `useEffect` hook. `useSyncExternalStore` also prevents the re-rendering problem that you are likely to face with `useEffect` whenever the state changes.

### Real-Time Use Cases

This interaction with external data sources that React doesn't control is precisely the gap `useSyncExternalStore` was designed to bridge safely and consistently. One project involved displaying real-time data from WebSockets while also respecting user preferences stored in localStorage.(no content)You could, for example, initiate a ResizeObserver in your subscribe function, then measure a ref as your value getter. The result is a less janky app.

### Common Pitfalls

Returning a new object/array reference from `getSnapshot` on every call, even if the data was identical, is the #1 cause of infinite render loops with this hook. Always cache and return the previous reference if the data hasn't changed.(no content)React will re-render the component if `getSnapshot` return value is different from the last time. This is why, if you always return a different value, you will enter an infinite loop and get this error.

### For a Canvas Game Loop

While the search didn't return specific articles about canvas game loops with `useSyncExternalStore`, the pattern would work well for:
- Subscribing to a game state store that updates on each animation frame
- Syncing React UI with external game engine state
- Handling real-time input events from canvas

Modern versions of libraries like Redux, Zustand, and Jotai often use `useSyncExternalStore` internally for their React bindings.

Would you like me to search more specifically for canvas game loop implementations or React game development patterns?


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "Three.js Babylon.js state management external store Zustand 2025"

Links: [{"title":"React State Management in 2025: What You Actually Need","url":"https://www.developerway.com/posts/react-state-management-2025"},{"title":"State Management in 2025: Context vs Zustand vs Jotai | Mikul Gohil","url":"https://www.mikul.me/blog/state-management-2025-context-zustand-jotai"},{"title":"How to use state management with react-three-fiber without performance issues - Questions - three.js forum","url":"https://discourse.threejs.org/t/how-to-use-state-management-with-react-three-fiber-without-performance-issues/61223"},{"title":"Compare Built-In vs. External State Management JS Libraries","url":"https://strapi.io/blog/built-in-vs-external-state-management-libraries"},{"title":"Babylon.js vs Three.js: The 360 Technical Comparison for Production Workloads - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/devin-rosario/babylonjs-vs-threejs-the-360deg-technical-comparison-for-production-workloads-2fn6"},{"title":"React State Management in 2025: Context API vs Zustand 🐻 - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/cristiansifuentes/react-state-management-in-2025-context-api-vs-zustand-385m"},{"title":"Using Babylon in Three JS context - Questions - Babylon.js","url":"https://forum.babylonjs.com/t/using-babylon-in-three-js-context/41625"},{"title":"State Management Trends in React 2025: When to Use Zustand, Jotai, XState, or Something Else - Makers' Den — Makers Den","url":"https://makersden.io/blog/react-state-management-in-2025"},{"title":"GitHub - pmndrs/zustand: 🐻 Bear necessities for state management in React","url":"https://github.com/pmndrs/zustand"},{"title":"Introducing Zustand (State Management) – Frontend Masters Blog","url":"https://frontendmasters.com/blog/introducing-zustand/"}]

Based on my search, here's what I found about Three.js, Babylon.js, state management, external stores, and Zustand in 2025:

## State Management with Three.js and Zustand

Users are combining react-three-fiber with Zustand for state management, representing 3D models in state through their basic props and rendering them from that state.(no content)When changes need to be made to 3D models, the state is changed and the models are updated automatically through hooks like useEffect.

## Babylon.js vs Three.js Architecture

Babylon.js, as a full engine, makes significant use of the CPU for scene management, frustum culling, and sophisticated internal state tracking. While this adds overhead, it results in better predictable frame times when dealing with thousands of objects.(no content)Three.js is naturally GPU-heavy by pushing rendering complexity to the shader level. This makes it excellent for lightweight experimentation or applications where rendering is the primary load, but complex scene logic can quickly become a CPU bottleneck if not managed with a custom ECS.

## Zustand as an External Store in 2025

In 2025, the React ecosystem has three dominant approaches for managing state: React Context (built-in), Zustand (external store), and Jotai (atomic state).(no content)Zustand is described as a fast, scalable, hook-first library that has matured into a production-grade standard.(no content)It is built on hooks, optimized for performance with minimal re-renders, offers middleware for persistence, logging, devtools, and async flows, and is now widely used in Web3 dApps, gaming dashboards, and Next.js server-side apps.(no content)Zustand is particularly recommended for performance-critical apps including gaming and 3D applications.

## Performance Considerations

External libraries introduce measurable overhead. Redux Toolkit adds approximately 45KB to your bundle, while Zustand contributes around 3KB. MobX sits at roughly 16KB.(no content)Context's weakness lies in its all-or-nothing re-render behavior, while Zustand and Jotai excel at granular updates.(no content)External libraries provide structure that scales with complexity. Redux selectors or Zustand's subscribe pattern deliver only the data a component requests, and normalized entities keep relationships predictable.

## Modern Patterns (2025)

Modern patterns combine React Server Components and frameworks like Next.js or Remix, using client-side stores (Zustand/Jotai) for interactivity after hydration while leveraging server-side data fetching.(no content)In 2025, many teams use Zustand + React Query (TanStack Query) together, handling both server state and client state efficiently.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "Entity Component System ECS WebGL Three.js game architecture"

Links: [{"title":"Entity-Component-System | Codecademy","url":"https://www.codecademy.com/article/a-frame-entity-component-system"},{"title":"Entity-Component-System – A-Frame","url":"https://aframe.io/docs/1.7.0/introduction/entity-component-system.html"},{"title":"👾 Entity - Component - System (ECS) | Web Game Dev","url":"https://www.webgamedev.com/code-architecture/ecs"},{"title":"aframe/docs/introduction/entity-component-system.md at master · aframevr/aframe","url":"https://github.com/aframevr/aframe/blob/master/docs/introduction/entity-component-system.md"},{"title":"entity-component-system · GitHub Topics · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/topics/entity-component-system?l=javascript"},{"title":"GitHub - dlarbi/3drpg: Entity-Component-System 3d RPG game engine","url":"https://github.com/dlarbi/3drpg"},{"title":"Introducing ECSY: an Entity Component System framework – Fernando Serrano","url":"https://fernandojsg.com/article/introducing-ecsy/"},{"title":"ECS Three.js Interop","url":"https://developers.meta.com/horizon/documentation/web/iwsdk-concept-three-basics-interop/"},{"title":"GitHub - daemonburrito/simple-enough-ecs: A Javascript game engine using an Entity-Component-System paradigm. **Pre-alpha**","url":"https://github.com/daemonburrito/simple-enough-ecs"},{"title":"Entity component system - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entity_component_system"}]

Here are the search results for "Entity Component System ECS WebGL Three.js game architecture":

## Entity Component System (ECS) for WebGL/Three.js Game Development

### What is ECS?

Entity–component–system (ECS) is a software architectural pattern used in video game development for the representation of game world objects. An ECS comprises entities composed from components of data, with systems that operate on those components.(no content)ECS prioritizes composition over inheritance. Every entity is not defined by a type hierarchy, but the components associated with it. Systems act globally over all entities that have the required components.

### Core Concepts

- **Entities**: Entities are container objects into which components can be attached. Entities are the base of all objects in the scene. Without components, entities neither do nor render anything, similar to empty `<div>`s.

- **Components**: Components are reusable modules or data containers that can be attached to entities to provide appearance, behavior, and/or functionality. Components are like plug-and-play for objects. All logic is implemented through components, and we define different types of objects by mixing, matching, and configuring components.

### Benefits of ECS

ECS offers greater flexibility when defining objects by mixing and matching reusable parts. It eliminates the problems of long inheritance chains with complex interwoven functionality and promotes clean design via decoupling, encapsulation, modularization, reusability.

### A-Frame: ECS + Three.js

A-Frame is a three.js framework with an entity-component-system (ECS) architecture. ECS architecture is a common and desirable pattern in 3D and game development that follows the composition over inheritance and hierarchy principle.(no content)A-Frame components can do anything. Developers are given permissionless innovation to create components to extend any feature. Components have full access to JavaScript, three.js, and Web APIs (e.g., WebRTC, Speech Recognition).

### ECSY Framework

After analyzing experience with three.js and A-Frame, and looking at the state of the art on game engines like Unity, ECSY was built using a pure Entity Component System architecture. The difference between a pure ECS like Unity DOTS, entt, or Entitas, and a more object-oriented approach, is that with a pure ECS approach, components just have data (without logic), and the logic resides in the systems.(no content)ECSY is engine/framework agnostic: you can use it directly with whichever 2D or 3D engine you prefer. Simple examples are provided for Babylon.js, three.js, and 2D canvas, with bindings and helper components planned for the most commonly used engines, starting with three.js.

### ECS + Three.js Interop

The interop system lets you leverage the best of both worlds: ECS's data-driven architecture for game logic, and Three.js's powerful rendering capabilities for visuals. Key Insight: ECS components hold the authoritative data. Three.js objects are synchronized views of that data.

### Other JavaScript ECS Implementations

There are JavaScript game engines with modular, take-what-you-want architecture with pluggable game loops, data structures, renderers, etc., with an emphasis on WebGL, using new JS data structures wherever possible, like TypedArrays.(no content)The 3drpg project started as an integration of THREE.js WebGL graphics into a JS ECS engine. It has 3 main classes for entities, components, and systems.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

## Entity Component System (ECS) for WebGL/Three.js Game Development

### What is ECS?

Entity–component–system (ECS) is a software architectural pattern used in video game development for the representation of game world objects. An ECS comprises entities composed from components of data, with systems that operate on those components.(no content)ECS prioritizes composition over inheritance. Every entity is not defined by a type hierarchy, but the components associated with it. Systems act globally over all entities that have the required components.

### Core Concepts

- **Entities**: Entities are container objects into which components can be attached. Entities are the base of all objects in the scene. Without components, entities neither do nor render anything, similar to empty `<div>`s.

- **Components**: Components are reusable modules or data containers that can be attached to entities to provide appearance, behavior, and/or functionality. Components are like plug-and-play for objects. All logic is implemented through components, and we define different types of objects by mixing, matching, and configuring components.

### Benefits of ECS

ECS offers greater flexibility when defining objects by mixing and matching reusable parts. It eliminates the problems of long inheritance chains with complex interwoven functionality and promotes clean design via decoupling, encapsulation, modularization, reusability.

### A-Frame: ECS + Three.js

A-Frame is a three.js framework with an entity-component-system (ECS) architecture. ECS architecture is a common and desirable pattern in 3D and game development that follows the composition over inheritance and hierarchy principle.(no content)A-Frame components can do anything. Developers are given permissionless innovation to create components to extend any feature. Components have full access to JavaScript, three.js, and Web APIs (e.g., WebRTC, Speech Recognition).

### ECSY Framework

After analyzing experience with three.js and A-Frame, and looking at the state of the art on game engines like Unity, ECSY was built using a pure Entity Component System architecture. The difference between a pure ECS like Unity DOTS, entt, or Entitas, and a more object-oriented approach, is that with a pure ECS approach, components just have data (without logic), and the logic resides in the systems.(no content)ECSY is engine/framework agnostic: you can use it directly with whichever 2D or 3D engine you prefer. Simple examples are provided for Babylon.js, three.js, and 2D canvas, with bindings and helper components planned for the most commonly used engines, starting with three.js.

### ECS + Three.js Interop

The interop system lets you leverage the best of both worlds: ECS's data-driven architecture for game logic, and Three.js's powerful rendering capabilities for visuals. Key Insight: ECS components hold the authoritative data. Three.js objects are synchronized views of that data.

### Other JavaScript ECS Implementations

There are JavaScript game engines with modular, take-what-you-want architecture with pluggable game loops, data structures, renderers, etc., with an emphasis on WebGL, using new JS data structures wherever possible, like TypedArrays.(no content)The 3drpg project started as an integration of THREE.js WebGL graphics into a JS ECS engine. It has 3 main classes for entities, components, and systems.

React Context vs Zustand vs Jotai vs Redux - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/saswatapal/do-you-need-state-management-in-2025-react-context-vs-zustand-vs-jotai-vs-redux-1ho"},{"title":"Zustand: A Simple and Scalable State Management Solution for React","url":"https://aexol.com/posts/zustand/"},{"title":"State Management in 2025: Context vs Zustand vs Jotai | Mikul Gohil","url":"https://www.mikul.me/blog/state-management-2025-context-zustand-jotai"},{"title":"Poimandres documentation","url":"https://docs.pmnd.rs/"},{"title":"State Management Trends in React 2025: When to Use Zustand, Jotai, XState, or Something Else - Makers' Den — Makers Den","url":"https://makersden.io/blog/react-state-management-in-2025"},{"title":"Zustand vs. Redux Toolkit vs. Jotai | Better Stack Community","url":"https://betterstack.com/community/guides/scaling-nodejs/zustand-vs-redux-toolkit-vs-jotai/"},{"title":"React State Management in 2025: Zustand vs. Redux vs. Jotai vs. Context","url":"https://www.meerako.com/blogs/react-state-management-zustand-vs-redux-vs-context-2025"}]

Here are the search results for "React Three Fiber external state management Zustand Jotai 2025":

## Key Findings

### Connection Between React Three Fiber, Zustand, and Jotai

Poimandres is a developer collective that hosts various OSS projects. Some of their famous projects are react-spring and react-three-fiber. They also provide three popular state managers, namely Zustand, Jotai, and Valtio.(no content)Zustand (German for "state") is a state management library created by Poimandres, the same team behind popular libraries like Jotai and React Three Fiber.

### State Management Recommendations in 2025

In 2025, the React ecosystem has three dominant approaches for managing state: React Context (built-in), Zustand (external store), and Jotai (atomic state).(no content)The choice between React Context, Zustand, and Jotai ultimately depends on your specific requirements, team preferences, and performance needs. Zustand provides the best balance of simplicity and performance for most applications, while Jotai excels when you need fine-grained reactivity and complex state relationships.

### Zustand Characteristics

Zustand is a tiny library primarily designed to create module state for React. It's based on an immutable update model, in which state objects can't be modified but always have to be newly created. Render optimization is done manually using selectors. It has a straightforward and yet powerful store creator interface.(no content)Zustand has become the popular middle ground - it's simple to use but surprisingly powerful. If you hate boilerplate code but still need solid state management, this might be your best bet.(no content)Zustand manages state outside of React's component tree, leading to better performance. The library is only a few kilobytes in size, making it a great choice for performance-conscious apps.

### Jotai Characteristics

Jotai is a small library for the global state. It's modeled after useState/useReducer and with what are called atoms, which are usually small pieces of state. Unlike Zustand, it is a component state, and like Zustand, it is an immutable update model.(no content)Jotai is an atomic state library where each atom is independent, so only subscribed components update.(no content)The second benefit of Jotai is non-global state. Atoms can be created and destroyed in the React component lifecycle. This is not possible with the multiple-Context approach, because adding a new state means adding a new Provider component.

### React Three Fiber State Management Considerations

Users have tried using Zustand with react-three-fiber to organize apps by representing 3D models in state through their most basic props, then rendering them from that state. Changes to 3D models are made through state changes, and models are updated automatically through hooks like useEffect.

### Decision Framework for 2025

The most important factor in choosing a state management solution in 2025 is not which library is "best" in absolute terms, but which one best aligns with your team's expertise, project requirements, and specific state characteristics.(no content)Quick decision framework: React Context is best for simple prop-drilling solutions in small to medium applications. Redux (RTK) is ideal for large enterprise applications with complex state logic. Jotai is perfect for applications with complex, atomic state relationships where granular re-renders matter.(no content)For modern React apps, you can combine client-side stores (Zustand/Jotai) for interactivity after hydration.

results:
Here are the search results for "React Three Fiber external state management Zustand Jotai 2025":

## Key Findings

### Connection Between React Three Fiber, Zustand, and Jotai

Poimandres is a developer collective that hosts various OSS projects. Some of their famous projects are react-spring and react-three-fiber. They also provide three popular state managers, namely Zustand, Jotai, and Valtio.(no content)Zustand (German for "state") is a state management library created by Poimandres, the same team behind popular libraries like Jotai and React Three Fiber.

### State Management Recommendations in 2025

In 2025, the React ecosystem has three dominant approaches for managing state: React Context (built-in), Zustand (external store), and Jotai (atomic state).(no content)The choice between React Context, Zustand, and Jotai ultimately depends on your specific requirements, team preferences, and performance needs. Zustand provides the best balance of simplicity and performance for most applications, while Jotai excels when you need fine-grained reactivity and complex state relationships.

### Zustand Characteristics

Zustand is a tiny library primarily designed to create module state for React. It's based on an immutable update model, in which state objects can't be modified but always have to be newly created. Render optimization is done manually using selectors. It has a straightforward and yet powerful store creator interface.(no content)Zustand has become the popular middle ground - it's simple to use but surprisingly powerful. If you hate boilerplate code but still need solid state management, this might be your best bet.(no content)Zustand manages state outside of React's component tree, leading to better performance. The library is only a few kilobytes in size, making it a great choice for performance-conscious apps.

### Jotai Characteristics

Jotai is a small library for the global state. It's modeled after useState/useReducer and with what are called atoms, which are usually small pieces of state. Unlike Zustand, it is a component state, and like Zustand, it is an immutable update model.(no content)Jotai is an atomic state library where each atom is independent, so only subscribed components update.(no content)The second benefit of Jotai is non-global state. Atoms can be created and destroyed in the React component lifecycle. This is not possible with the multiple-Context approach, because adding a new state means adding a new Provider component.

### React Three Fiber State Management Considerations

Users have tried using Zustand with react-three-fiber to organize apps by representing 3D models in state through their most basic props, then rendering them from that state. Changes to 3D models are made through state changes, and models are updated automatically through hooks like useEffect.

### Decision Framework for 2025

The most important factor in choosing a state management solution in 2025 is not which library is "best" in absolute terms, but which one best aligns with your team's expertise, project requirements, and specific state characteristics.(no content)Quick decision framework: React Context is best for simple prop-drilling solutions in small to medium applications. Redux (RTK) is ideal for large enterprise applications with complex state logic. Jotai is perfect for applications with complex, atomic state relationships where granular re-renders matter.(no content)For modern React apps, you can combine client-side stores (Zustand/Jotai) for interactivity after hydration.

Web search results for query: "WebGL game state management 2025 architecture patterns"

Links: [{"title":"WebGL Game Development: Complete Browser Gaming Guide 2025","url":"https://generalistprogrammer.com/tutorials/webgl-game-development-complete-browser-gaming-guide-2025"},{"title":"Game Development Patterns and Architectures in JavaScript by Olayinka Atobiloye","url":"https://gitnation.com/contents/game-development-patterns-and-architectures-in-javascript"},{"title":"Game Design Patterns: Complete Guide to Scalable Game Architecture","url":"https://generalistprogrammer.com/game-design-patterns"},{"title":"Enjoyable Game Architecture","url":"https://chickensoft.games/blog/game-architecture"},{"title":"React Architecture Pattern and Best Practices in 2025 - GeeksforGeeks","url":"https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/reactjs/react-architecture-pattern-and-best-practices/"},{"title":"Design Patterns for Games: State Pattern | by Oreste Leone | Better Programming","url":"https://betterprogramming.pub/design-patterns-for-games-state-pattern-97519e0b9165"},{"title":"State · Design Patterns Revisited · Game Programming Patterns","url":"https://gameprogrammingpatterns.com/state.html"},{"title":"(PDF) An Architecture For Game State Management Based On State Hierarchies","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/237439949_An_Architecture_For_Game_State_Management_Based_On_State_Hierarchies"},{"title":"JavaScript Game Development: Master Core Techniques for 2025 🎮✨","url":"https://playgama.com/blog/general/javascript-game-development-core-techniques-for-browser-based-games/"},{"title":"Best JavaScript and HTML5 game engines (updated for 2025) - LogRocket Blog","url":"https://blog.logrocket.com/best-javascript-html5-game-engines-2025/"}]

Here are the search results for "WebGL game state management 2025 architecture patterns":

## Key Architecture Patterns for WebGL Game State Management

### Entity Component System (ECS)
Component-based architecture patterns in WebGL game development improve code organization and reusability across projects. Implementing entity-component-system (ECS) patterns with Three.js or other frameworks enables flexible, modular game object management. This architectural approach scales well for complex JavaScript 3D games with diverse gameplay mechanics and interactive elements.(no content)Entity component systems are popular in JavaScript game development for representing games as entities and their components as behavior on data.(no content)For larger games, the Entity-Component-System (ECS) architecture offers significant advantages, separating entity data from behavior logic. This approach facilitates easier feature additions and provides better performance for games with numerous interactive elements.

### State Machine Architecture
Modern JavaScript 3D games leverage WebGL's state machine architecture, where rendering state persists between draw calls. Efficiently managing state changes—like shader programs, textures, and blend modes—significantly impacts performance.(no content)The State Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that allows an object to change its behavior based on its internal state to respond to different inputs and events dynamically and flexibly.

### Game Loop Patterns
Game loops control the flow of the game and updating its state, with different architectures like fixed time step and variable time step. Decoupling game logic from rendering improves performance and flexibility, allowing for independent updates.(no content)The game loop is a core feature of game development that continuously cycles through steps of processing user inputs, updating the game state, and rendering the game visuals. This loop runs repeatedly, allowing the game to progress and respond to user interactions and other game events.

### State Stack Pattern
This pattern naturally handles complex state hierarchies common in modern games. When the player opens settings from the pause menu, the settings state is pushed onto the stack. Closing settings pops the stack, returning to the pause menu. Closing the pause menu pops again, returning to gameplay. The stack maintains the entire state history without manual tracking.

### Object Pooling
The object pool pattern reuses objects instead of constantly creating and destroying them, dramatically reducing garbage collection pressure and memory allocation overhead. This pattern is critical for frequently spawned objects like bullets, particles, enemies, and effects that would otherwise cause frame rate stutters from memory management.

### Render State Management
Render state management significantly impacts performance in complex browser game development projects. Minimizing state changes, batching similar rendering operations, and organizing draw calls by material properties reduces GPU pipeline stalls. Professional JavaScript 3D games implement sophisticated scene management systems that automatically sort and batch rendering operations for optimal performance.

### High-Level Architecture Patterns
Large-scale game projects require high-level architectural patterns that organize entire codebases. While games don't always fit traditional MVC or MVVM patterns cleanly, adapted versions of these architectures provide structure for complex games with extensive UI, networking, and persistent systems. MVC separates game state (Model) from rendering (View) and input/logic (Controller). This pattern works well for turn-based games, strategy games, and games with complex UI systems where separating business logic from presentation is valuable.

### Best Practices
Prefer dependency injection and service locator patterns over singletons for better testability and flexibility.(no content)The ideal architecture should define abstraction layers to organize code modules, provide an organization system for the files and assets in the codebase, and allow each "unit" of code to be tested in isolation.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
# Poimandres State Management Libraries

Based on the documentation page, Poimandres offers several state management solutions:

## Core Libraries

**Zustand** - Described as "a small, fast and scalable bearbones state-management solution, it has a comfy api based on hooks"

**Jotai** - "a primitive and flexible state management library for React"

**Valtio** - Makes "proxy-state simple for React and Vanilla"

## Integration with React Three Fiber

While the page doesn't explicitly detail integration specifics, these libraries are part of the broader Poimandres ecosystem. Zustand and Jotai are particularly popular for managing state in React Three Fiber applications because they:

- Use React hooks, making them compatible with React-based rendering
- Offer lightweight state solutions that work well with 3D rendering performance requirements
- Integrate naturally with other pmndrs libraries like React Three Fiber and Drei

The Poimandres documentation hub presents these as complementary tools within their interconnected library ecosystem, though deep integration examples aren't provided on this index page.

content.content:
# Entity Component System (ECS) in Game Development

Based on the Web Game Dev resource, ECS is a code architecture pattern for games, though the page notes it still needs comprehensive documentation on what ECS is and how it compares to other paradigms.

## Key Characteristics

ECS separates game logic into three core concepts:
- **Entities**: Objects in your game world
- **Components**: Data attached to entities
- **Systems**: Logic that operates on entities with specific components

## Popular JavaScript/TypeScript Libraries

The page lists several maintained ECS libraries for web games:

- **bitECS** (730 stars) - High performance option
- **Miniplex** (600 stars) - Includes React bindings
- **Koota** (200 stars) - New library with React integration
- **ECSY** (1k stars) - Established library

## Learning Resources

The page references several educational materials about ECS, including comparisons to object-oriented programming paradigms, benchmarking tools, and advanced concepts like entity relationships and data-oriented programming architecture.

The TODO section indicates the documentation is still developing coverage of core ECS concepts, practical implementation patterns, and architectural best practices.

Links: [{"title":"How to use state management with react-three-fiber without performance issues - Questions - three.js forum","url":"https://discourse.threejs.org/t/how-to-use-state-management-with-react-three-fiber-without-performance-issues/61223"},{"title":"Create a game with React Three Fiber: Part 1 - Zustand - Wawa Sensei","url":"https://wawasensei.dev/tuto/react-three-fiber-tutorial-hiragana-katakana-game"},{"title":"Zustand: Forget about other state managers | by Burak Erdem | Medium","url":"https://falsemech.medium.com/zustand-forget-about-other-state-managers-281f269382a7"},{"title":"GitHub - pmndrs/zustand: 🐻 Bear necessities for state management in React","url":"https://github.com/pmndrs/zustand"},{"title":"Introducing Zustand (State Management) – Frontend Masters Blog","url":"https://frontendmasters.com/blog/introducing-zustand/"},{"title":"State Management in 2025: When to Use Context, Redux, Zustand, or Jotai - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/hijazi313/state-management-in-2025-when-to-use-context-redux-zustand-or-jotai-2d2k"},{"title":"Kill Da Corona - my first game ✌🏼 - Showcase - three.js forum","url":"https://discourse.threejs.org/t/kill-da-corona-my-first-game/15498"},{"title":"Getting Started with Three.js for Your First 3D Game | MoldStud","url":"https://moldstud.com/articles/p-create-a-simple-3d-game-with-threejs-step-by-step"},{"title":"GitHub - freddykrunn/three.gf: Game development Engine/Framework based on Three.Js","url":"https://github.com/freddykrunn/three.gf"},{"title":"zustand examples - CodeSandbox","url":"https://codesandbox.io/examples/package/zustand"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about using Zustand with Three.js for game state management:

## Zustand + Three.js Game State Implementation

### Tutorial Resources

Wawa Sensei has a tutorial on creating a game with React Three Fiber where they set up a physics engine, load 3D models, render Japanese text in 3D, and create a game engine using Zustand for state management.

### Performance Considerations

Developers using react-three-fiber have found success using Zustand to organize their apps by representing 3D models in state through their most basic props, then rendering them from that. When you want to change something about the 3D models, you change the state and the models are updated automatically through hooks like useEffect.

### Why Zustand Works Well with Three.js

Zustand is hook-oriented and requires no provider context to wrap with, which is a big plus considering performance.(no content)The subscribe function allows components to bind to a state-portion without forcing re-render on changes. Best combined with useEffect for automatic unsubscribe on unmount. This can make a drastic performance impact when you are allowed to mutate the view directly.

### Basic Zustand Store Example

Zustand stores are created with a simple hook pattern. You can use the hook anywhere without providers, and components will re-render selectively on state changes.

### State Management in 2025

Zustand is considered "great for medium to large applications needing simplicity without sacrificing power - the sweet spot for many teams."(no content)While Redux remains a strong choice for large enterprise applications, Zustand has emerged as the versatile middle ground that works well for most projects.

### Example Projects

CodeSandbox has several Zustand examples including a Three-fiber-template, raycast-vehicle implementation using use-cannon/cannon-es, 3d-terrain-r3f, and a mini Minecraft implementation.

### Key Features for Game Development

Zustand doesn't care if your actions are async or not - you can just call set when you're ready. The `set` function allows function updates, and you have access to state outside of it through `get`.(no content)Zustand doesn't care where or when the set function is called. You're free to have async methods in your store, which call set after a fetch.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "game loop requestAnimationFrame Zustand React integration pattern"

Links: [{"title":"Writing a Run Loop in JavaScript & React | by Luke Millar | projector_hq | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/projector-hq/writing-a-run-loop-in-javascript-react-9605f74174b"},{"title":"React - Frame/Game loop using requestAnimationFrame · GitHub","url":"https://gist.github.com/whoisryosuke/bb2c90131b3e7fd56709743061fbf597"},{"title":"Anatomy of a video game - Game development | MDN","url":"https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Games/Anatomy"},{"title":"Using requestAnimationFrame with React Hooks | CSS-Tricks","url":"https://css-tricks.com/using-requestanimationframe-with-react-hooks/"},{"title":"Exploring Virtual Reality with React Game Engines","url":"https://www.dhiwise.com/post/exploring-virtual-reality-with-react-game-engines"},{"title":"Performant Game Loops in JavaScript | Aleksandr Hovhannisyan","url":"https://www.aleksandrhovhannisyan.com/blog/javascript-game-loop/"},{"title":"Game Mechanics in Javascript - The Game Loop","url":"https://lawrencewhiteside.com/courses/game-mechanics-in-javascript/the-game-loop/"},{"title":"Using requestAnimationFrame in React for Smoothest Animations","url":"https://blog.openreplay.com/use-requestanimationframe-in-react-for-smoothest-animations/"},{"title":"react-native.requestAnimationFrame JavaScript and Node.js code examples | Tabnine","url":"https://www.tabnine.com/code/javascript/functions/react-native/requestAnimationFrame"},{"title":"How to use requestAnimationFrame with React","url":"https://how.dev/answers/how-to-use-requestanimationframe-with-react"}]

Here are the results for your search on "game loop requestAnimationFrame Zustand React integration pattern":

## Key Findings

### Using requestAnimationFrame with React Hooks

Animating with requestAnimationFrame should be easy, but if you haven't read React's documentation thoroughly, you will probably run into a few gotchas. The key recommendations are: Pass an empty array as a second parameter for useEffect to avoid it running more than once, pass a function to your state's setter function to make sure you always have the correct state, and use useRef for storing things like the timestamp and the request's ID.

### Basic Game Loop Pattern in React

The useEffect hook is perfect for implementing the game loop in a React app. It allows you to perform side effects in your components, such as setting up an interval to update the game state and render the screen. The hook can be used to initiate the game loop when the component mounts and clean it up when the component unmounts, preventing memory leaks and ensuring optimal performance.

### requestAnimationFrame Fundamentals

The browser provides an API to synchronize your loop to the actual frames it's going to draw. It's called requestAnimationFrame and it works just like setTimeout but it's timed to the refresh rate of your monitor.(no content)Like setTimeout and setInterval, requestAnimationFrame takes a callback that will run at a later point in time. But in this case, that point in time is the next render opportunity, after the browser is done processing macro- and micro-tasks and just before it repaints. These render callbacks don't interfere with other tasks, and they're enqueued at a regular interval: the refresh rate of the user's display. For example, a 60 Hz display schedules requestAnimationFrame callbacks so that 60 of them run every second (60 FPS).

### Handling Different Refresh Rates

If you were to just run this code as-is, it may seem fine on whatever display you're testing with. On most modern devices, that's likely going to be a 60 Hz display. But if someone were to play your game on a 120 Hz display, the requestAnimationFrame callbacks would be invoked twice as frequently. This isn't a problem for draw() functions, but since the function responsible for updating physics would also be called twice as often, a player on a 120 Hz display would move twice as fast as a player on a 60 Hz display.(no content)To fix this, you can compute the animation budget of each frame (1000 ms / 60 = 16.67 ms per frame for 60 FPS). Then keep track of the timestamp when the previous frame executed, calculate the amount of real time that elapsed since then, and only run the game physics if at least that much time has elapsed. This ensures that player physics never updates faster than 60 FPS even on higher refresh rate displays.

### Best Practices for React Integration

It is a better pattern to only initialize requests once, keep them spinning by the animate function and then cleanup once when the component unmounts. To make sure the useEffect hook runs only once, you can pass an empty array as a second argument to it.(no content)Performing animations with requestAnimationFrame in React introduces intricacies distinct from Vanilla JavaScript. It involves a good understanding of state, props, and React's life-cycle methods. Efficient side effects management is essential for consistent performance and behavior.

### Browser Tab Handling

An important thing to remember for managed platforms like the web is that your loop may stop execution for significant periods of time. This could occur when the user unselects your tab and the browser sleeps (or slows) its requestAnimationFrame callback interval. Some choices to handle this include: considering the gap as a "pause" and skipping the time.

---

**Note:** The search results didn't return specific information about Zustand integration with game loops. For Zustand specifically, you would typically:
1. Keep your game state in a Zustand store
2. Use `getState()` and `setState()` directly in your game loop (outside React's render cycle) for better performance
3. Subscribe to state changes in React components using Zustand's selectors

## Key Findings

### Using requestAnimationFrame with React Hooks

Animating with requestAnimationFrame should be easy, but if you haven't read React's documentation thoroughly, you will probably run into a few gotchas. The key recommendations are: Pass an empty array as a second parameter for useEffect to avoid it running more than once, pass a function to your state's setter function to make sure you always have the correct state, and use useRef for storing things like the timestamp and the request's ID.

### Basic Game Loop Pattern in React

The useEffect hook is perfect for implementing the game loop in a React app. It allows you to perform side effects in your components, such as setting up an interval to update the game state and render the screen. The hook can be used to initiate the game loop when the component mounts and clean it up when the component unmounts, preventing memory leaks and ensuring optimal performance.

### requestAnimationFrame Fundamentals

The browser provides an API to synchronize your loop to the actual frames it's going to draw. It's called requestAnimationFrame and it works just like setTimeout but it's timed to the refresh rate of your monitor.(no content)Like setTimeout and setInterval, requestAnimationFrame takes a callback that will run at a later point in time. But in this case, that point in time is the next render opportunity, after the browser is done processing macro- and micro-tasks and just before it repaints. These render callbacks don't interfere with other tasks, and they're enqueued at a regular interval: the refresh rate of the user's display. For example, a 60 Hz display schedules requestAnimationFrame callbacks so that 60 of them run every second (60 FPS).

### Handling Different Refresh Rates

If you were to just run this code as-is, it may seem fine on whatever display you're testing with. On most modern devices, that's likely going to be a 60 Hz display. But if someone were to play your game on a 120 Hz display, the requestAnimationFrame callbacks would be invoked twice as frequently. This isn't a problem for draw() functions, but since the function responsible for updating physics would also be called twice as often, a player on a 120 Hz display would move twice as fast as a player on a 60 Hz display.(no content)To fix this, you can compute the animation budget of each frame (1000 ms / 60 = 16.67 ms per frame for 60 FPS). Then keep track of the timestamp when the previous frame executed, calculate the amount of real time that elapsed since then, and only run the game physics if at least that much time has elapsed. This ensures that player physics never updates faster than 60 FPS even on higher refresh rate displays.

### Best Practices for React Integration

It is a better pattern to only initialize requests once, keep them spinning by the animate function and then cleanup once when the component unmounts. To make sure the useEffect hook runs only once, you can pass an empty array as a second argument to it.(no content)Performing animations with requestAnimationFrame in React introduces intricacies distinct from Vanilla JavaScript. It involves a good understanding of state, props, and React's life-cycle methods. Efficient side effects management is essential for consistent performance and behavior.

### Browser Tab Handling

An important thing to remember for managed platforms like the web is that your loop may stop execution for significant periods of time. This could occur when the user unselects your tab and the browser sleeps (or slows) its requestAnimationFrame callback interval. Some choices to handle this include: considering the gap as a "pause" and skipping the time.
